input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:

Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

<current_datetime>2026-05-08T00:57:13.862Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference - Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated this as a success.

Let me look at their project files to understand the setup.

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>

    <!-- Treat discovering zero tests as success (exit code 0 instead of 8). -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>

    <!-- Treat discovering zero tests as success (exit code 0 instead of 8). -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.content: The `--minimum-expected-tests` rejects `0`. The log reveals `--ignore-exit-code 8` is the correct option for this scenario.

arguments.old_str:
<!-- Treat discovering zero tests as success (exit code 0 instead of 8). -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Exit code 8 = "no tests found"; treat it as success for assemblies that
         may legitimately contain zero tests due to conditional compilation. -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText: The `--minimum-expected-tests` option doesn't accept 0 - it requires a "non-zero positive integer". But there's another option I see: `--ignore-exit-code 8` which ignores specific exit codes. Let me use that instead.

data.content:
**The fix:** Add `--ignore-exit-code 8` via `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` in your csproj.

Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns **exit code 8** when zero tests are discovered — unlike VSTest which returned 0. The `--ignore-exit-code 8` flag tells the runner to map that specific exit code back to 0, restoring the VSTest behavior for assemblies that legitimately produce no tests under certain build configurations.
